sentences.text: Well, we-- my mother and my sister, the three of us.
And as long as we were together, we thought things would be OK.
And we'll stay there.
And no matter how hard it is and how bad it is, at least we have a roof over our heads, and we are together.
And we know that our father is safe.
And now comes 1941, and the, the war breaks out between Germany and the Soviet Union.
The Judenrat-- they set up a Judenrat.
And they were-- that was a group of men that were probably chosen by the Nazis.
I wouldn't really know because when I came, it was all in place.
And the young Jewish men were put in uniforms.
And they were the Jewish police that were in charge of order in the ghetto.
Some of them-- most of them were volunteering for that job because being a policeman would entitle you and your family to extra food and some privileges.
We cannot sit in judgment because these were not normal times.
And to be able to save your family from starvation, you did things that would not-- you would not normally do.
No, they took us to go to work in the fields to harvest potatoes and grain for the farmers.
So this way, we were getting extra food.
And that's how we survived.
Because, remember, we had no money.
We had no other income.
And I would not allow my mother to go to work.
There's no way.
So my sister and I were going out for, to work.
And that would be-- we kind of could get by.
I will-- one-- you know, Jews survive on laughter, even if it's bad.
No matter how bad it is, there's always somewhere, sometime where you have to, to look at the lighter side.
They-- in our rations, there was so many grams of jam that you would be given.
Now, the merchant already would take out some of the jam and put water in because it went by weight.
And he sold the jam on the black market.
We, of course, didn't have money to buy anything on the black market.
So when he gave-- sold you your ration of jam, that was already watered down a little.
Now, I was growing up.
And I was craving for sweet.
And the only thing we had was this jam.
So when my mother wouldn't be home, I would take a spoon of jam and put a spoon of water.
But the more jam I took out and the more water I put in, it became slowly juice.
And I thought my mother will never notice.
And then-- and she was saving the jam for the holidays because she wanted to bring some normalcy into our lives.
For instance, we had a Seder in the ghetto.
We had services for Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur in our room where we had people come, and it all clandestine because we were not allowed to congregate more than two, three people.
But we had prayers.
And a Torah scroll was brought in.
And we had services for yontif.
And my mother was saving the jam to bake a cake.
And when she made the dough-- and she's now taking the jar with the jam, and she finds there is nothing-- no jam, but just some red juice.
She threw the jar at me.
But she didn't hit me.
She hit the door.
And the jar broke.
And the jam was all over the door and the floor.
And I thought it was funny.
But this was, this was the life in the ghetto.
Oh, yes, all the time.
There would-- a Jew would-- I remember the men would wear their coats with their collars up and would hide their beards so they-- and pull down their hats.
And they would run for prayers because you couldn't stop the Jews from praying.
I mean, the religious life went on, even, even with the Nazis being there.
And they would see somebody running.
They would grab him by his beard, cut it off, and, you know, ridicule him and, and kick him and beat him in the street.
And you always hear, heard this thing.
In the neighboring town, they hung four Jews for just leaving the ghetto.
There was all, all the time things happening.
But, as I said, you tried to stay in your place, in your room, go out as little as possible.
And then those awful rumors that were circulating-- most of what we survived on was on rumors of hope, that the war is ending and that the Nazis are being defeated.
It was nothing to it, but I guess we needed this.
Yeah, I'm trying to think.
I don't remember it right now.
It was-- because I was a stranger in that area.
But I was later in camp with some girls from there.
No, we only knew my paternal grandmother came with us.
Kaufman, Ida Kaufman and [NON-ENGLISH] Kaufman.
Well, she was Steinitz before.
She came with us to Jaworzno.
But my maternal grandmother was shipped, because she lived in another town.
She was shipped to a town in Poland called Olkusz.
So my sister and I would borrow a bicycle from somebody, take off our armband-- the Star of David-- and we would go and visit with her.
Of course it was.
But when you're young, you don't care.
You want to see you grandmother, you go and see her.
We also wanted to take some food.
As little as we had, we wanted to make sure that she had some food.
She was an older lady in her 70s and alone.
And there was no way that we could bring her because we were all registered.
There was no such thing as movements.
So life in the ghetto was hell.
But you tried to survive every day and hope for the end of the war.
Well, you had Jewish doctors.
But there was no hospital that would accept anybody.
And there was no medication.
So the doctor, except for sympathy, couldn't do much for you.
For instance, when someone died, the cemetery was outside of town.
Only the Chevra Kadisha would take the body and go to the cemetery and bury and not leave even a mark on the grave.
Because Rabbi Plaut's uncle died in that ghetto.
He's buried in Jaworzno in the cemetery.
And I can't find the grave.
I would love to have a photo for Rabbi Plaut of his uncle's grave.
And there is none.
There's no-- I don't know.
There was children that were taught.
There were schools.
But this was happening in basements.
Or they had rooms where they put a cabinet in front of the door, that if the Nazis would come in, they wouldn't know there was another room behind.
But Jewish life continued.
Well, it actually was the same when I came, and it stayed the same.
It wasn't much to go down anymore.
It had not much to deteriorate.
I think it hit the bottom already.
There wasn't as many people as, let's say, in the Warsaw ghetto, that there was epidemics where the people got sick.
They died.
Children got sick.
There was no medication.
They died.
Babies were born, they died.
But malnutrition, and the related sickness to that.
But it wasn't epidemics because there weren't-- we weren't rat infested or anything like that.
It wasn't that populated.
In 1941, the war broke out between Germany and the Soviet Union.
Well, suddenly we didn't know what happened to my father.
Because we didn't know when the Nazis moved on and into Russia, into the Soviet Union, whether he moved back with the Soviets or stayed behind.
We began to worry about his well-being again.
He no longer was safe.
One Saturday morning, there was a knock on the door, a quiet knock, very early.
And I opened the door.
And there was a peasant standing outside.
And as I looked again, startled why he would knock on our door, I recognized my father.
When the Soviets moved back, they asked him to come with them.
They really liked him.
He worked there in a factory and worked his way up from a janitor to running the office, learned Russian.
He was very good in languages.
And he worked himself up.
And they begged him to come with-- right into the Soviet Union.
He missed us so much that he could no longer stay separated from us and decided to come to be reunited.
Well, because we knew where he was, so we wrote to him.
And it took a long time before he got our mail.
But he did know where we were and had our address.
He came in, closed the door.
We looked the door.
And the door was never again left open, first thing changed.
I cannot describe the reunion with my mother, with him, with me, with my sister.
And instantly, the fear, the joy of being reunited and the terrible fear what this all means and how are we going to handle it.
He was not registered in the ghetto.
And you had to be registered.
And you couldn't suddenly appear.
And we had no money to pay off the police, the Jewish police, or the Judenrat to put his name down.
We were strangers.
We were German Jews.
We weren't trusted.
We couldn't go and say, I pay you, and register my father who just arrived.
So we moved-- there was a clothes cabinet in the other room because it used to be a book, a book, where books were held.
And then we put a rod, a curtain rod, between the wall and that closet, that cabinet, and put a sheet in front of it and put a chair behind it.
And that's where my father spent day after day, every day.
Because not even my grandmother, not even his mother, knew that he came back.
It was such a secret that he couldn't even see his mother.
Because she was an older lady, and we couldn't trust her to keep it a secret.
In the joy of seeing her son, she might have said something.
And you never knew how it would get out.
So my mother, in order that we can have him there legally, asked me to go back to our hometown, to Silesia, and get a certificate that he moved, that he left the town, that he moved.
See, in Europe, you have to register when you move.
You can't just pick and move somewhere else.
So you have to register.
When you move from one town to another, you go in City Hall and register.
And then you register in the other town.
So I took off my, my armband, got on a train, and went back home.
And I had to change trains in the city of Auschwitz-- Auschwitz?
And there I sat and waited for another train, for the train that would take me to my hometown.
Of course, I could not appear to look scared or worried.
Because this is where the soldiers that were coming from the Eastern Front were changing over as well.
So I had to pretend that I'm German.
Of course, was no problem.
I spoke fluent German.
No.
Yes.
It was a border.
This was Poland, occupied by the Germans.
I was going to German proper.
I had nothing-- no identification.
Because, as you know, any identification that a Jew had in Germany from 1934 on had a J stamped.
And our name-- 'Sarah' was added to our name.
I suddenly had a middle name.
It was now Gerde Sarah Steinitz.
So even if I had identification, I wouldn't dare to show it to anyone.
So I had to talk myself across the border.
And the only way to do it is to sit in the same compartment that the German soldiers were sitting.
And when they came for control of passports, they pretended that I am their girlfriend.
And I played that game.
And then when the train crossed the border, I got out, and I took the next train-- as long as I was across the border.
I arrived in my hometown.
I went to some friends.
As a matter of fact, it was our maid.
And I told her I came to my town at night so no one sees me because everybody knew me.
And I told her what happened.
And we hid with her some of my father's clothes, because my mother never give up hope.
And so she left some things so that when my father comes back, he should at least have a suit and something to wear.
And so I went and picked up some clothes for my father.
And I told her that I need this certificate for him.
So she went for me to the town council.
And she was able to get it for me.
I didn't have to go.
And then at night, I took the package there that-- my clothes for my father and a certificate.
And I went back.
We were all German.
We didn't associate with Polish people.
My mother wouldn't.
She couldn't speak Polish.
These were the Germans that lived in Poland, like me.
We were Germans living in Poland.
Elizabeth Jochimsky.
I went with her sister to school.
And I came back again the way I went down.
No.
I was not scared that she would betray.
These were people who we could trust.
There were people that you could trust.
You wouldn't ask them to hide you, because you would endanger them.
And this you didn't want to do.
But you would ask them to hide certain things for you or do you a favor.
As a matter of fact, my grandmother would get people from Bielszowice come to Jaworzno and bring her food.
German friends that lived in Bielszowice would come to the ghetto and bring her food parcels.
That is the community we lived in.
It was a different community than Poland.
Well, because there were all kind of businesses.
And it was not just Jews there.
There were still some Poles living in that ghetto, too.
You just had a curfew.
You were not allowed to leave.
But there was not a physical wall erected.
You didn't dare to leave, because if you wanted to live, you better stay in.
Well, I did leave.
For me, it was, was important to try and have my father there legally so at least he could leave the place.
And then I went to the authorities in Jaworzno, and I registered him, that he's now a resident of Jaworzno.
And that's when he could leave the room.
And that's when we could tell my grandmother.
But to have him behind that curtain all day-- my sister had a boyfriend.
And he would come and visit.
And they would sit in the room, and my father would be behind the curtain.
And he did not know that anyone was in the room.
And those two young people were sitting and talking.
And he was there.
But he didn't know that he was there.
And my sister could not even tell him, or-- pretend that they are alone and they were just-- yeah.
Any visitor, anybody that knocked on our door, we would say, just a moment, I'm just getting dressed.
And then my father would disappear behind the curtain.
And then I would open the door and let them in.
We had friends that came to visit.
Yes.
No, we were the refugees.
My sister made friends because she's a people person.
But my father told me to take care of my mother.
And I never left her for one minute, ever.
When she went to visit friends, I went with her.
Wherever she went, I never left her, because she, well, she always threatened to commit suicide.
And I was afraid she would.
And I would have this terrible responsibility for her safety because my father told me to take care of her.
If you tell a child, take care of your mother, that's what you do.
And my sister made many friends.
I didn't.
I was always with my mother.
But then my father was able to go out already.
And then we could tell his mother very gently because that would've been a big shock for her.
1942, in February, after they've taken most of the young men out of the ghetto into camps, they began to take women, girls, young, young women.
Yeah, there was a quota.
They went to the Judenrat and say, we want a hundred men from this ghetto.
And it was up to the Jewish police to round up the young people.
So, at that time, you stopped going outside altogether.
Because you could leave your house, go shopping, go anywhere, visit a friend, and they would catch you in the street, and goodbye.
And your family wouldn't even know that you disappeared.
But that was not such a great idea, because you get rations for the family.
If there were five people in the family, and there was one young man that they had to report to go to camp, and he didn't report because he was hiding, you would get now food for four people.
Now, five people didn't have enough to survive on the rations you got.
But four people, for sure, couldn't survive.
That was their weapon.
Food was their weapon.
So some got away with it because if they had money, they could go and buy on the black market.
But Jews in Poland were not rich.
Not everybody had money to buy on the black market.
So when they already had the men taken out of the ghetto, then they turned to the women, to young women.
So in February, my sister was taken away.
She was picked up by the Jewish police.
And actually, she was replacing someone.
She was not on the list.
But we were strangers in this community.
So somebody that had money paid off.
But they need still so many bodies.
So my sister was taken, even if she was not on the list.
And that's how she found herself in a concentration camp.
And she was taken to Sudetenland.
They were allowed to write once a month.
And so we knew where she was.
She was in Oberaltstadt, a company, a spinning mill-- company was called Ettrich.
And at least we knew that she was alive, that she was in a camp where she was working.
And this is how it was.
There was no way that you would go out and demonstrate.
People do not understand this, that you live in such fear.
We were powerless.
What was there we could do?
We couldn't hide.
No one would hide us.
We couldn't run away.
No one-- no country would accept us.
If you had money, you could go underground and get maybe Aryan papers and live as a non-Jew.
Some people did.
But then you had to know how to do this.
How-- three women-- my mother with two children, she would have to hide all of us.
You couldn't hide one.
It was, it was just impossible.
It was impossible.
At that time, there were no extermination camps that we knew of.
We did not know that there were towns that were-- all the people were taken in the forest and shot.
We did not know that there was synagogues where people were herded into the synagogues, gasoline poured over them, and burned with the people inside.
We did not know that.
And maybe the people in the ghetto knew.
But remember, we were not part of the community.
We were the German refugees.
We were not trusted.
We were separate.
The group of the 35 families, we kind of stuck together.
And we really didn't know.
The only thing we knew, my grandmother's friend, in early 1942, came with a parcel.
It was food.
And he was an engineer on a railroad.
And he said to my grandmother, hide any place you can.
I am taking trains into Auschwitz.
There is a camp.
I'm leaving empty.
I'm going in with cars, cattle cars, full of people.
And I'm leaving empty.
And when I come back the next time, I don't know where the people are.
There should be thousands there.
Nobody is there.
They must be killing them.
Try to run.
Try to hide.
That's the first time that we heard an actual account of Auschwitz, only from this German friend that my grandmother had.
So my sister was now in camp.
And they were still catching young girls, mostly at night.
They would come, bang on your door, and take out whoever was-- so I was hiding in a barn every night with a friend of mine.
Her sister was caught together with my sister.
So they were in the same camp.
And I was hiding in a barn with, with her sister.
Every night, we were sitting there in the barn, all winter.
The mice were our company.
We were counting how many were jumping over the straw.
And it was Passover.
And the last night of Passover, April 11, my mother said to me she heard rumors that the camps are full.
They don't need anybody anymore.
